Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd try to set up a schedule at the beginning of each week, but with 16 online courses and everything else going on, it never really stuck. I usually ended up studying whenever I could grab a few hours, often late at night when my anxiety wasn't as bad. I'd write out plans in my notebook but rarely followed them completely because something always came up or I'd get overwhelmed by how much there was to do.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. Some weeks I'd barely touch certain courses because I was drowning in deadlines for others. When assignments were coming up, I'd cram everything into a few intense sessions, which probably wasn't the healthiest approach. Regular study times were kind of a luxury I couldn't afford with my schedule and limited resources.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline that got me to log in, if I'm being real. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about something due and that would snap me into action. Other times I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which happened more often than I'd like to admit. The anxiety of not knowing what I was behind on was sometimes worse than just checking.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became more routine, usually early in the semester when I was still optimistic. I'd try to check in every morning while eating breakfast, just scrolling through announcements. But that habit fell apart pretty quickly once the workload piled up and I started feeling overwhelmed. It's hard to maintain routines when you're constantly putting out fires.
Q5: The reading materials and written content were most useful for me since that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and go through them at my own pace, highlighting and taking notes. Discussion posts were also helpful because seeing how other students understood things gave me different perspectives. The structured readings made complex topics more manageable when everything else felt chaotic.
Q6: I often skipped or delayed watching the video lectures because they took so long and I couldn't always afford the data to stream them properly. Some of the optional supplementary materials I ignored because I was just trying to survive the required stuff. I know I probably missed out on helpful content, but when you're stretched thin, you have to prioritize what's going to be graded.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. If I knew something would help me pass, I'd make time for it no matter what. I also valued things that I could complete relatively quickly because small wins helped with my anxiety. Anything that felt like busywork without clear purpose got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, honestly. Required ones had this weight of consequence hanging over them, so they always took priority. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that would be nice to do' but then reality would hit and I'd focus on survival. Sometimes I felt guilty about skipping optional content because I knew it could help, but there just weren't enough hours.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download readings and PDFs to my phone so I could study during commutes or when my internet was spotty. I borrowed textbooks from friends and spent hours with physical notes spread across my bed. My notebook is filled with summaries and practice problems that never showed up in any system.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours going through downloaded materials or rewriting notes by hand to help things stick. I'd quiz myself using flashcards I made or work through problems on paper. Group study sessions with classmates happened in person, not online. The LMS would show nothing during these times, but I was genuinely working hard.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd open a reading once, download it, and then study from the downloaded version for days. Same with lecture slides—I'd access them once to save them, then review them multiple times offline. My internet isn't always reliable, so working offline became necessary. The LMS only saw that initial access, not the hours I spent with the material afterward.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. My anxiety spiked and I was barely sleeping, trying to keep up with all 16 courses. There were days when just opening the LMS felt overwhelming because I knew I'd see how behind I was. The CBT sessions helped me cope somewhat, but the sheer volume of work was crushing. Financial stress at home didn't help either.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. The workload was genuinely impossible to manage perfectly with so many courses. When I was exhausted or confused about material, I'd sometimes just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Family responsibilities and my part-time work meant the LMS often got neglected even when I wanted to engage more.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through content just to feel like I was doing something, but my mind was elsewhere. I'd have readings open while worrying about other deadlines or personal stuff. Sometimes I'd watch a video but realize at the end that I hadn't actually processed any of it. Those sessions felt hollow, like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed a lot of discussion posts and some assignments because the anxiety of starting them was paralyzing. When something felt too big or confusing, avoiding it temporarily felt like relief, even though I knew it would make things worse. Sometimes I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was in a better headspace, but tomorrow brought its own problems.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire relationship with the LMS. I'd barely touch a course until something was due, then frantically try to catch up. It wasn't ideal, but it was the only way I could manage so many courses. The deadline pressure actually helped cut through my anxiety sometimes because there was no choice but to act.
Q17: Feedback was motivating when it was positive—it reminded me I could actually do this. But when feedback was critical or I got a low grade, it would send my anxiety through the roof and make me avoid that course for a while. Quick feedback helped me adjust, but waiting weeks for grades made it hard to know if I was on track.
Q18: The course structure mattered a lot. Courses with clear weekly expectations were easier to manage than ones where everything was vague. When modules were organized logically, I could at least see what needed to happen even if I couldn't do it all. Poorly structured courses added to my confusion and made my anxiety worse.
Q19: Flexible deadlines would have helped me enormously, but most courses didn't offer that. Having all materials downloadable was great for my situation. What made things harder was when courses required constant online presence or synchronous activities that clashed with my work schedule. Clear instructions made engagement easier; ambiguity made me freeze up.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me, desperately trying to figure out what's going on but struggling to actually complete things. You might log in repeatedly checking requirements, downloading materials, reading announcements, but still not submit work because you're overwhelmed or confused. High activity doesn't mean high productivity when you're drowning.
Q21: That definitely reflects part of my experience. I did a lot of learning offline that never showed up in the system. Someone might access materials once, master them through personal study, and perform well on exams without much LMS activity. The platform only captures one slice of how people actually learn.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the emotional state behind the numbers. They can't tell if someone was genuinely learning or just anxiously scrolling. The human experience of struggling and persevering doesn't show up in data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just passing. It's when you're curious and thinking about concepts even outside of study time. For me, engagement also means showing up despite the anxiety and overwhelm, even when it's hard. It's a mental and emotional investment that goes beyond clicking through content.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show the panic attacks before logging in or the relief when I finally submitted something. My resilience, my offline studying, my genuine desire to learn despite low motivation—none of that appears in the data. It also wouldn't show that being happy sometimes was my way of coping with how stressed I actually was.
